replies.author: @hanelyp1
replies.text: Inertia.  The chain segments want to travel in straight lines.  Along the lower arc the chain is constrained to follow the perimeter of the socket, being held to the sprocket by 2 idlers.  But once it passes into the section where it has slack it flies in a straight line until the returning length pulls it back to the sprocket.

author: @mrgriff6122
text: How does it work with a longer chain?

author: @andrewharpin6749
text:
There's a proposal to use one many miles long (IRO 200-300) as a space launch system, accelerate the "rope" up to hypersonic speeds,  then you "hook" your spacecraft and accelerate up to a significant portion of orbital velocity, with the upper part of the rope mostly out of the atmosphere and release at the top, with some form of propulsion getting you the last part to orbital velocity.

Acting as a Stage 1 of a rocket.

Doesn't require a tether as strong as a space elevator and so more practical with current materials.

Obviously there are risks, but it appears to be currently the most practical concept thus far to replace honking great rockets for getting into orbit.
